introduction./summary: Understand that PMP assesses a project manager's decision-making competence, not the ability to memorize a book.
introduction./objectives/0: Understand the structure of the PMP exam based on the current Examination Content Outline and how to allocate your time.
introduction./objectives/1: Distinguish among PMI, PMP, PMBOK, Exam Content Outline, and training materials.
introduction./objectives/2: Establish a study roadmap, an error log, and a strategy for practicing scenario-based questions.
introduction./opening/0: Many people begin studying for PMP by reading PMBOK from cover to cover and trying to memorize process names. After a few weeks, they know many terms but remain confused by scenario-based questions because they do not know what knowledge PMI is testing, which action must be taken first, or what role each source plays.
introduction./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as its recurring story. The project has a business sponsor, a sales team, an operations team, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show that every decision affects multiple stakeholders. You will encounter the same group of people in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision to be made.
introduction./opening/2: PMP assesses the ability to apply a project management mindset in a specific context. ECO describes the competencies being tested, PMBOK provides a system of principles and practices, and training materials help structure the learning journey. No source should be treated as a set of answers to memorize. As you study, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of reasoning: understand the context, verify the facts, communicate with the right people, assess the impact, select an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

introduction./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach for Real-World Work
introduction./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Define your learning objective and current level of experience instead of copying someone else's schedule. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
introduction./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Use the 15 lessons as a map, matching each topic to ECO and the relevant PMI source. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.

introduction./sections/5/title: A Reasoning Framework for Selecting Actions
introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In scenario-based questions, keywords are only cues. “Urgent” does not automatically mean escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean immediate implementation, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the event's status, its impact, the decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If you do not understand what the question is testing, return to the task/enabler in ECO before opening multiple books. Then check whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has work to do within their authority.
introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If you answer correctly but cannot explain why, still record it in the error log because it may have been a correct guess. Then check whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has work to do within their authority.
introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If two sources use different terminology, prioritize context and official definitions instead of debating the wording. introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If low scores are concentrated in one pattern, relearn that pattern through contrasting cases before answering hundreds more questions. introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If exam information may have changed, check the current PMI website instead of relying on old notes. introduction./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong option often respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. Therefore, PMP assesses judgment in context more than the ability to memorize a list of processes.
introduction./comparison/0/useWhen: When the study scope and assessment milestones are clear, you can create a weekly plan, baseline the study time, and track variances.
introduction./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration work for the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, with a business sponsor, a sales team, an operations team, an internal development team, and an external vendor, is baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through the designated authority.
introduction./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and handled through change control.
introduction./comparison/1/useWhen: When you do not yet know your weaknesses, study in short cycles: read, answer questions, receive feedback, and then prioritize the knowledge backlog.
introduction./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on real feedback.
introduction./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without structure; objectives, roles, Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
introduction./comparison/2/useWhen: Keep the 15-lesson framework stable while adjusting the amount of practice based on the error log and the results of each session.
introduction./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
introduction./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to run as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
introduction./caseStudy/context: The sponsor of the order platform project wants the entire team to “study PMP so the project follows standards,” but each person interprets “standards” differently. The technical team thinks of processes, sales thinks of speed, and operations is concerned with stability.
introduction./caseStudy/steps/0: The Project Manager establishes a shared understanding that PMP is not a set of processes to be applied directly to the project.
introduction./caseStudy/steps/1: The entire team distinguishes the project's outcome from the output of the training activities.
introduction./caseStudy/steps/2: The learners map the 15 topics and connect each topic to a real issue they are facing.
introduction./caseStudy/steps/3: After the first practice session, the team classifies errors instead of reporting only the total score.
introduction./caseStudy/steps/4: The study plan is adjusted based on data but is not tied to a fixed deadline.
introduction./caseStudy/outcome: The team shares a common language for learning objectives, knows which material to use for each question, and tracks progress through the quality of decisions. Learning becomes a feedback loop instead of a race to finish reading every book.
introduction./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Treating one book as the sole syllabus.
introduction./commonMistakes/0/better: Use ECO as the map and combine the appropriate sources for each topic.
introduction./commonMistakes/0/reason: PMP tests multiple approaches and competencies, not the memorization of one book.
introduction./commonMistakes/1/better: Also record correct answers given with low confidence.
introduction./commonMistakes/1/reason: Correct guesses conceal knowledge gaps and can easily become errors in other scenarios.
introduction./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Setting the same fixed study deadline for everyone.
introduction./commonMistakes/2/better: Track individual progress and make data-driven adjustments.
introduction./commonMistakes/2/reason: Foundational knowledge, available time, and learning pace vary; the course must be usable at any time.
introduction./examMindset/0: Read the context before selecting a tool.
introduction./examMindset/1: Prioritize assessment and collaboration before taking forceful action.
introduction./examMindset/2: Do not infer information that is not provided in the question.
introduction./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and communicating with the relevant people before using forceful measures, except when safety, legal, or ethical concerns require immediate protection.
introduction./examMindset/4: Select an action appropriate to the point in time asked about: the first action is often to assess or clarify; the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
introduction./recap/0: The core of PMP Fundamentals is turning knowledge into decisions with context, an owner, and observable results.
introduction./recap/1: Key artifacts include an 8–12-week Study Plan, an Incorrect Answer Log, and a Score Tracker by domain, but documentation is valuable only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
introduction./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
introduction./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for selecting answers reflexively.

introduction./practice/title: Create a Personal PMP Study Map
introduction./practice/context: You have 15 lessons, multiple sets of materials, and a large question bank. You do not know which book to start with and can devote only a few short time slots each week.
introduction./practice/learnerRole: You are both the learner and the primary manager of your study plan.
introduction./practice/inputs/0: The list of 15 lessons in the VTI curriculum.
introduction./practice/inputs/1: The results of a short diagnostic session or a self-assessment of your experience.
introduction./practice/inputs/2: PMBOK, Agile Practice Guide, Scrum Guide, ECO, and handout sources.
introduction./practice/inputs/3: The amount of time you can realistically study on a regular basis.
introduction./practice/tasks/0: Write a learning objective as an outcome instead of a number of pages to read.
introduction./practice/tasks/1: Assign each source a specific purpose.
introduction./practice/tasks/2: Create a five-column error log and one example for each error type.
introduction./practice/tasks/3: Choose a way to assess progress after every three lessons.
introduction./practice/deliverable: A one-page learning map containing objectives, sources, study cadence, error taxonomy, and checkpoints.
introduction./practice/guidance/0: Start with what you want to be able to do when you encounter a scenario-based question.
introduction./practice/guidance/1: Limit each source to one role to avoid unfocused reading.
introduction./practice/guidance/2: Checkpoints should be based on your ability to explain and on error patterns, not only the total score.
introduction./practice/guidance/3: Leave room to adjust the plan when new data becomes available.
introduction./practice/rubric/0/evidence: The objective describes an observable competency or behavior.
introduction./practice/rubric/0/importance: A clear outcome helps you select the right learning activities.
introduction./practice/rubric/1/evidence: Each source clearly states whether it is used to look up concepts, scope, or ways of working.
introduction./practice/rubric/1/importance: Reduces duplicate reading and confusion about the source of truth.
introduction./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Includes the error type, missed cue, correct principle, and corrective action.
introduction./practice/rubric/2/importance: Turns incorrect answers into improvement data.
introduction./practice/rubric/3/evidence: Includes checkpoints and adjustment rules, with no mandatory deadline.
introduction./practice/rubric/3/importance: Makes the course suitable for different learning paces.
introduction./practice/sampleAnswer/0: Objective: explain why an action is appropriate for the context, role, and timing.
introduction./practice/sampleAnswer/1: ECO is used to define the scope; PMBOK is used to understand the system; Agile/Scrum is used for adaptive ways of working.
introduction./practice/sampleAnswer/2: The error log includes knowledge, reading, mindset, time, and careless.
introduction./practice/sampleAnswer/3: After every three lessons, review the three most frequent error patterns and then revise the learning backlog.
introduction./practice/commonMistakes/0: Planning by page count without a target competency.
introduction./practice/commonMistakes/1: Consulting every source for the same question.
introduction./practice/commonMistakes/2: Tracking only the average score without classifying errors.
introduction./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Which part of the plan shows that your understanding is deepening, rather than merely that you are studying longer?
introduction./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: What cues will prompt you to change the plan?

fundamentals./summary: Build a foundation in value, value delivery systems, life cycles, governance, and tailoring an approach to the project environment.
fundamentals./objectives/0: Differentiate among projects, programs, portfolios, products, and operations.
fundamentals./objectives/1: Understand the 6 principles and 7 performance domains of PMBOK 8 and how they relate to delivery.
fundamentals./objectives/2: Choose Predictive, Adaptive, or Hybrid based on the level of uncertainty and the ability to obtain feedback.
fundamentals./opening/0: A project can finish on time and deliver all required functionality yet still fail if users do not adopt it or the expected business benefits do not materialize. The problem often begins when the team focuses only on a list of deliverables without understanding the value delivery system in which the project operates.
fundamentals./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as its recurring scenario. The project has a sponsor responsible for the business, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The example is familiar enough to keep beginners from getting stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show that a decision always affects multiple stakeholders. You will encounter the same participants in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
fundamentals./opening/2: Project management fundamentals involve viewing a project as a temporary system that creates intentional change. Projects, programs, portfolios, products, and operations have different life cycles, decision rights, and success measures. A Project Manager must understand these boundaries to avoid optimizing the project at the expense of the product or organization. As you read, do not try to turn every situation into a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

fundamentals./sections/5/title: A Framework for Choosing an Action
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requests it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the event's status, its impact, the decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If the objective describes only a deliverable, ask who receives the value and which metric must change. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has work to perform within their authority.
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If the scope belongs to a long-term product, clarify the temporary project component and identify the owner after handoff. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has work to perform within their authority.
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If requirements are uncertain, increase the feedback cadence instead of trying to elaborate assumptions in detail. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has work to perform within their authority.
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If the process is too burdensome, tailor it while retaining mandatory controls. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has work to perform within their authority.
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If a change creates a local optimization, assess its system-wide impact before approving it. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has work to perform within their authority.
fundamentals./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong option usually respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. Therefore, PMP assesses contextual judgment more than the ability to memorize lists of processes.
fundamentals./comparison/0/useWhen: Appropriate when deliverables, regulations, and dependency chains are relatively stable.
fundamentals./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration work for the order management platform implementation across multiple departments—with a sponsor responsible for the business, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor—is baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through the defined authority.
fundamentals./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
fundamentals./comparison/1/useWhen: Appropriate when the solution must be discovered through frequent user feedback.
fundamentals./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on actual feedback.
fundamentals./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without discipline; objectives, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
fundamentals./comparison/2/useWhen: Appropriate when compliance or infrastructure work is stable but the experience and features are still evolving.
fundamentals./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested through iterations; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
fundamentals./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not let the two approaches operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
fundamentals./caseStudy/context: The order platform project is described as “replacing the legacy system,” but the sponsor's actual goal is to shorten processing time and reduce data re-entry errors. If the team only delivers the software, the outcome may not be achieved because the processes and operational capabilities have not changed.
fundamentals./caseStudy/steps/0: The sponsor and Project Manager rewrite the objective from “system go-live” to reducing order processing time.
fundamentals./caseStudy/steps/1: The team establishes that the project ends after handoff, while the product team continues to optimize the product.
fundamentals./caseStudy/steps/2: Data and integration constraints are separated from the user experience work that requires experimentation.
fundamentals./caseStudy/steps/3: Governance defines who makes decisions about the baseline, backlog, and integration points.
fundamentals./caseStudy/steps/4: The benefits owner assumes responsibility for measuring outcomes after the project closes.
fundamentals./caseStudy/outcome: The team no longer equates completing outputs with creating value. The life cycle and governance are tailored to the characteristics of the work, while responsibility for benefits continues after go-live.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Treating the project and the product as the same thing.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/0/better: Separate the project life cycle from the product and operations.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/0/reason: They have different durations and owners.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Choosing Agile because you want to move quickly.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/1/better: Choose the approach based on uncertainty, feedback, and constraints.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/1/reason: Speed is not the only criterion, and Agile cannot correct an unclear objective.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/2/better: Track adoption, outcomes, and benefits as well.
fundamentals./commonMistakes/2/reason: A deliverable is only a means of creating value.
fundamentals./examMindset/1: Assess system-wide impacts instead of optimizing locally.
fundamentals./examMindset/2: Value matters more than the quantity of outputs.
fundamentals./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting the relevant stakeholders before taking forceful action, except when an immediate response is required to protect safety, legal compliance, or ethics.
fundamentals./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time asked about: the first action is usually to assess or clarify, while a final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
fundamentals./recap/0: The central idea of Project Management Fundamentals is to turn knowledge into decisions with clear context, ownership, and observable results.
fundamentals./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Business case, Benefits management plan, Project charter, and Tailoring approach, but documents have value only when they support communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
fundamentals./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
fundamentals./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for choosing answers reflexively.

fundamentals./practice/title: Tailor the Approach for the Order Project
fundamentals./practice/context: Infrastructure, security, and data migration have fixed requirements; the sales interface is not yet clearly defined and requires feedback every two weeks.
fundamentals./practice/learnerRole: You are the Project Manager preparing a proposal for the sponsor.
fundamentals./practice/inputs/0: The objective is to reduce order processing time by 30%.
fundamentals./practice/inputs/2: The UX team can review with users once every two weeks.
fundamentals./practice/inputs/3: Operations requires documentation and training before go-live.
fundamentals./practice/tasks/0: Differentiate the project output from the business outcome.
fundamentals./practice/tasks/1: Choose an approach for each group of work.
fundamentals./practice/tasks/2: Identify three governance points and two integration points.
fundamentals./practice/tasks/3: Assign a benefits owner for the period after project closure.
fundamentals./practice/deliverable: A tailoring canvas that describes the outcome, the Predictive component, the Adaptive component, integration points, and decision rights.
fundamentals./practice/guidance/0: Do not choose a single label for the entire project before classifying the work.
fundamentals./practice/guidance/1: Each governance point must be tied to an actual decision.
fundamentals./practice/guidance/2: Each integration point needs inputs, readiness criteria, and an owner.
fundamentals./practice/guidance/3: The benefits owner should belong to the unit capable of sustaining the outcome.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/0/evidence: Includes an operational metric beyond go-live.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/0/importance: Prevents the team from declaring success too early.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/1/evidence: Each component is selected based on uncertainty and feedback.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/1/importance: Tailoring must be based on context.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Identifies who makes decisions about the baseline, backlog, and release.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/3/evidence: An owner is assigned to measure benefits after project closure.
fundamentals./practice/rubric/3/importance: Value must be sustained after handoff.
fundamentals./practice/sampleAnswer/0: The output is the system and migrated data; the outcome is a 30% reduction in processing time.
fundamentals./practice/sampleAnswer/1: Infrastructure and migration use Predictive; UX uses iterations; releases use shared governance.
fundamentals./practice/sampleAnswer/2: The sponsor approves the business scope, the Product Owner prioritizes the backlog, and the change authority addresses impacts to the baseline.
fundamentals./practice/sampleAnswer/3: The Operations manager is the benefits owner and measures the metric during operations.
fundamentals./practice/commonMistakes/0: Calling the entire project Hybrid without defining the boundaries.
fundamentals./practice/commonMistakes/1: Using too many ceremonies that do not produce decisions.
fundamentals./practice/commonMistakes/2: Failing to assign responsibility after handoff.
fundamentals./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Which tailoring decision reduces the greatest risk?
fundamentals./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: If user feedback is no longer regular, how would you adjust the approach?

stakeholder-communication./summary: Identify the right people, understand their interests and influence, and then design two-way communication that builds commitment instead of merely distributing information.
stakeholder-communication./objectives/0: Analyze stakeholder power, interest, impact, influence, and attitude.
stakeholder-communication./objectives/1: Develop a communication plan based on information needs, channels, frequency, and responsibilities.
stakeholder-communication./objectives/2: Address stakeholder resistance by listening, clarifying the causes, and co-creating solutions.
stakeholder-communication./opening/0: Stakeholders rarely object simply because an email is missing. They may fear losing control, facing a greater workload, seeing their KPI decline, or bearing risks the project has not identified. Sending more reports without understanding their interests and concerns often makes the situation worse.
stakeholder-communication./opening/1: Throughout this lesson, we will use the example of a project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales team, operations, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The example is familiar enough to keep beginners from getting stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show how one decision can affect many parties. You will encounter the same group of people in later lessons; what changes will be the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
stakeholder-communication./opening/2: Stakeholder management is the process of identifying people who influence or are affected by the project, understanding their expectations, and designing an appropriate level of engagement. Communication is a two-way cycle that creates understanding and action; evidence that an email was sent does not prove that its message was understood. As you read, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

stakeholder-communication./sections/4/title: A step-by-step approach for real work
stakeholder-communication./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Update the stakeholder register when roles, power, or attitudes change. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
stakeholder-communication./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Compare the current engagement level with the level needed for the upcoming decision. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
stakeholder-communication./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Select the message, channel, timing, and level of detail based on the recipient's needs. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
stakeholder-communication./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Create a feedback loop to confirm understanding, commitment, and action. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
stakeholder-communication./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Monitor responses and adjust the engagement plan instead of repeating the same communication approach. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
stakeholder-communication./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically mean escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean immediate implementation, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the event's current status, its impact, who has decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
stakeholder-communication./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If a stakeholder unexpectedly objects, identify the cause and needs before escalation. Then check whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
stakeholder-communication./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If the information is complex or consensus is needed, choose interactive communication instead of relying only on push communication. Then check whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
stakeholder-communication./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If the recipient does not act, verify their level of understanding and decision-making authority instead of assuming they are irresponsible. Then check whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
stakeholder-communication./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If expectations conflict, bring the trade-offs and success criteria into the same discussion. stakeholder-communication./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If a new stakeholder emerges, reassess engagement instead of merely adding them to the email distribution list. Then check whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.

stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/context: The head of operations objects to the release despite receiving weekly reports. Only during a direct conversation does the Project Manager learn that the department has no one available to train the night shift and fears being held accountable if order processing is disrupted.
stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/steps/0: The Project Manager does not argue by email but schedules a private discussion with the head of operations.
stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/steps/1: The two parties separate facts, concerns, decision authority, and missing support.
stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/steps/2: Night-shift training and a rollback drill are added to the release plan.
stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/steps/3: The sponsor receives a summary of impacts, options, and owners instead of all the technical details.
stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/steps/4: After the drill, operations confirms readiness through a checklist and direct feedback.
stakeholder-communication./caseStudy/outcome: The objection is converted into actionable transition requirements. Stakeholders participate in the appropriate decisions, the technical team does not have to send more useless reports, and the release has evidence of readiness.

stakeholder-communication./commonMistakes/2/reason: Silence does not prove that the recipient understood or committed.

team./summary: Build cross-functional, psychologically safe, self-organizing teams; servant leadership removes impediments and develops capabilities.
team./objectives/0: Differentiate leadership from management and select a style based on the team's maturity.
team./objectives/1: Develop a team charter, working agreement, and ground rules.
team./objectives/2: Provide coaching, mentoring, training, and feedback based on specific needs.
team./opening/0: A project team may bring together all the necessary experts yet still perform poorly if roles overlap, people do not feel safe raising risks, or every decision must wait for the Project Manager. People-related problems often appear as schedule delays, but their root cause lies in how the team collaborates.
team./opening/1: Throughout this lesson, we will use the implementation of an order management platform across multiple departments as a recurring scenario. The project involves a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The scenario is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show that one decision always affects multiple parties. You will encounter the same participants in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
team./opening/2: Leading a team means enabling people to understand the objectives, boundaries, decision authority, and approach to resolving disagreements. Servant leadership does not mean doing the team's work; it means removing impediments, protecting focus, developing capabilities, and delegating authority at an appropriate level. As you read, do not try to turn every situation into a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, select an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

team./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach for Real-World Work
team./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Observe behavior and its impact instead of labeling people. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
team./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Speak directly with the parties to identify the root cause, needs, and unclear boundaries. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
team./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Review the team charter, working agreement, and governance. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
team./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Facilitate agreement within the team on criteria and solutions that fall within its delegated authority. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
team./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Monitor behavior and outcomes after the intervention while coaching the team to handle similar situations independently in the future. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean governance can be ignored. Determine the event's status, its impact, the decision authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If the conflict remains safe, bring the relevant people together for a direct discussion. Then assess whether the action produces better information, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If roles are unclear, clarify decision authority before evaluating attitudes. Then assess whether the action produces better information, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If the team has sufficient capability, delegate authority over how the work is performed while maintaining boundaries around the outcome. Then assess whether the action produces better information, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If an ethical or safety violation occurs, protect the affected people and escalate according to established procedures. Then assess whether the action produces better information, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If performance declines, coach based on the root cause instead of increasing micromanagement. Then assess whether the action produces better information, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
team./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong option usually respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. PMP therefore evaluates judgment in context more than the ability to memorize process lists.
team./comparison/0/useWhen: Roles, the responsibility matrix, and escalation paths are usually described clearly in the plan.
team./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration components of the order management platform implementation across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, are baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through defined authorities.
team./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
team./comparison/1/useWhen: A cross-functional team self-organizes within the boundaries of the product goal, sprint goal, and Definition of Done.
team./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on actual feedback.
team./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without structure; objectives, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
team./comparison/2/useWhen: Architecture approval authority may follow governance, while the team independently decides on the detailed solution within each iteration.
team./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
team./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
team./caseStudy/context: The internal architect and the vendor's technical lead continually argue about the design. Both believe they have final approval authority, causing the team to stop work and junior members to avoid expressing their views.
team./caseStudy/steps/0: The Project Manager meets separately with the two team leads to hear the facts and understand the impact.
team./caseStudy/steps/1: A governance review reveals that advisory and decision authority have not been clearly documented.
team./caseStudy/steps/2: The sponsor confirms the decision owner, and the team jointly defines the design criteria.
team./caseStudy/steps/3: The working agreement is updated with an approach for constructive challenge and a decision deadline.
team./caseStudy/steps/4: The Project Manager monitors two decision cycles and then gradually reduces involvement.
team./caseStudy/outcome: The conflict is resolved by addressing its root cause—unclear authority—rather than choosing a winner. The team feels safe challenging ideas, makes decisions faster, and learns how to resolve disagreements independently.
team./commonMistakes/0/wrong: The Project Manager decides every disagreement.
team./commonMistakes/0/better: Enable the team to resolve disagreements within clear boundaries.
team./commonMistakes/0/reason: The team cannot mature if every decision is taken away from it.
team./commonMistakes/1/better: Address conflict early and focus on the issue.
team./commonMistakes/1/reason: Avoided disagreements often reappear as delays or poor quality.
team./commonMistakes/2/wrong: A servant leader agrees to every request.
team./commonMistakes/2/better: Serve the objectives and the team's growth within established boundaries.
team./commonMistakes/2/reason: Servant leadership remains accountable for outcomes and governance.
team./examMindset/0: Protect the team without concealing problems.
team./examMindset/1: Remove the blocker first, then teach the team to remove blockers independently over time.
team./examMindset/2: Address the issue with the people directly involved.
team./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the root cause and speaking with the relevant people before taking stronger action, except in safety, legal, or ethical situations that require immediate protection.
team./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time addressed by the question: the first action is often to assess or clarify; the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
team./recap/0: The central lesson of Team is to turn knowledge into decisions with context, an owner, and observable results.
team./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Team charter, Working agreement, Skills matrix, and Recognition plan, but documentation has value only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
team./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
team./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for selecting answers reflexively.
team./selfCheck/0: When two team members have a technical disagreement, should the PM decide for them or facilitate agreement on the criteria?
team./selfCheck/1: Can you explain this topic to a new colleague using an example without rereading the definition?
team./selfCheck/2: Can you identify the information to collect, the people to consult, and the artifact to update in the sample project case?
team./selfCheck/3: If the approach changes from Predictive to Agile, where will the sequence of actions and decision authority change?

team./practice/context: Two technical leads are disputing approval authority, meetings are running long, and three architecture decisions are pending.
team./practice/learnerRole: You are the Project Manager coordinating the internal team and vendor.
team./practice/inputs/0: The current RACI lists both individuals only as Responsible.
team./practice/inputs/1: The sponsor does not want to be involved in technical details.
team./practice/inputs/2: The team has sufficient expertise to evaluate the options.
team./practice/inputs/3: One decision that affects security requires a mandatory compliance review.
team./practice/tasks/0: Analyze the root cause and impact of the conflict.
team./practice/tasks/1: Identify which decisions the team can make independently and which require governance.
team./practice/tasks/2: Draft a working agreement for the design challenge session.
team./practice/tasks/3: Develop a follow-up plan after two decision cycles.
team./practice/deliverable: A team intervention plan covering the root cause, decision authority, ground rules, and monitoring metrics.
team./practice/guidance/0: Do not describe anyone as difficult to work with; describe observable behavior.
team./practice/guidance/1: Separate routine technical decisions from compliance decisions.
team./practice/guidance/2: Ground rules must allow disagreement while preventing personal attacks.
team./practice/guidance/3: Metrics should measure decision time and collaboration quality.
team./practice/rubric/0/evidence: The plan identifies unclear authority and its specific impact.
team./practice/rubric/0/importance: Only an intervention that addresses the root cause will be sustainable.
team./practice/rubric/1/evidence: The team independently makes the appropriate decisions; compliance decisions have a reviewer.
team./practice/rubric/2/evidence: The plan defines how to present views, challenge ideas, and finalize decisions.
team./practice/rubric/3/evidence: Metrics and a review time are specified.
team./practice/rubric/3/importance: Confirms that the intervention produces genuine improvement.
team./practice/sampleAnswer/0: Root cause: The RACI does not identify who is Accountable; the impact is a delay in three decisions.
team./practice/sampleAnswer/1: The Architecture forum selects the design based on agreed criteria; a security exception requires compliance approval.
team./practice/sampleAnswer/2: Challenge the facts, not the person; the decision owner finalizes the decision after hearing all relevant views.
team./practice/sampleAnswer/3: After two weeks, measure decision lead time and collect anonymous team feedback.
team./practice/commonMistakes/0: Immediately choose one technical lead as the winner.
team./practice/commonMistakes/1: Ask the sponsor to decide every detail.
team./practice/commonMistakes/2: Use overtime hours as a measure of commitment.
team./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Are you resolving the disagreement or taking authority away from the team?
team./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: What signals indicate that the team can handle a similar situation independently next time?

predictive-planning./summary: Translate business needs into a scope baseline, a feasible schedule, and a controllable integrated plan.
predictive-planning./objectives/0: Develop requirements, the scope statement, the WBS, and the scope baseline.

predictive-planning./opening/0: In a Predictive project, a detailed plan has no value if requirements are not traceable, the WBS omits deliverables, or the schedule is merely a list of dates estimated by one person. A good baseline is not an attractive plan; it is the shared basis for forecasting and managing change.
predictive-planning./opening/1: Throughout this lesson, we will use a project that implements an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The example is familiar enough to keep beginners from getting caught up in technical details, yet complex enough to show how one decision can affect many parties. You will encounter the same participants in later lessons; what changes will be the perspective, facts, and type of decision required.
predictive-planning./opening/2: Predictive planning translates a business need into bounded scope, decomposed work, logical dependencies, estimates supported by assumptions, and approved baselines. Every number must be traceable to a requirement, an estimating method, and an accountable person. As you read, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of reasoning: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, select an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or document.

predictive-planning./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Collect requirements and establish traceability to the business need. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
predictive-planning./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Define scope, state exclusions clearly, and decompose deliverables using the WBS. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.

predictive-planning./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. predictive-planning./comparison/0/useWhen: Use scope, schedule, and cost baselines when changes are costly and requirements are sufficiently stable.
predictive-planning./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration components of the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external vendor, are baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through defined authority.
predictive-planning./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat a baseline as an immutable document; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
predictive-planning./comparison/1/useWhen: Plan only enough for the near-term horizon when requirements remain highly volatile.
predictive-planning./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on real feedback.
predictive-planning./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as an unstructured way of working; objectives, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
predictive-planning./comparison/2/useWhen: Baseline integration milestones; manage feature discovery through a backlog and iterations.

predictive-planning./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
predictive-planning./caseStudy/context: The sponsor requires the order platform to go live within four months. The initial schedule contains hundreds of activities but does not account for the time the vendor needs to provide the API or identify who will accept the data migration.

predictive-planning./caseStudy/steps/3: Network analysis shows that the migration rehearsal is on the critical path.
predictive-planning./caseStudy/steps/4: The sponsor approves the baseline with contingency for known risks.
predictive-planning./caseStudy/outcome: The plan reflects the actual execution logic and constraints, not merely desired dates. The team knows which activities have float, which dependencies require monitoring, and which changes must go through change control.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Organizing the WBS by organizational structure.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/0/better: Decompose by deliverable and apply the 100% rule.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/0/reason: The WBS must encompass the entire scope, not reflect organizational silos.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Treating every delayed activity as a project delay.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/1/better: Review float and the critical path.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/1/reason: Schedule impact depends on the network, not merely an individual variance.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Including management reserve in the cost baseline.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/2/better: Keep contingency reserve and management reserve separate according to their roles.
predictive-planning./commonMistakes/2/reason: The two reserves address different types of uncertainty and have different authorization requirements.
predictive-planning./examMindset/0: A baseline may be changed only through change control.
predictive-planning./examMindset/1: Develop estimates with the people who will perform the work and document the assumptions clearly.
predictive-planning./examMindset/2: Do not shorten the schedule before analyzing the critical path.
predictive-planning./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and communicating with relevant stakeholders before using forceful measures, except when immediate action is required to protect safety or address legal or ethical concerns.
predictive-planning./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time described by the question: the first action is usually to assess or clarify; the final solution comes only after sufficient facts and authority are available.
predictive-planning./recap/0: The core of Predictive: Planning is turning knowledge into decisions with context, an owner, and an observable result.

predictive-control./summary: Execute the plan, perform integrated change control, measure performance, and close the project with verified evidence.
predictive-control./objectives/0: Apply issue, change, configuration, and knowledge management.
predictive-control./objectives/1: Use Earned Value to interpret variances and forecasts.
predictive-control./objectives/2: Distinguish Validate Scope, Control Quality, and Close Project or Phase.
predictive-control./opening/0: Once a project is underway, pressure to “act now” often causes the team to skip impact analysis or documentation updates. A change requested verbally by the sponsor can cause the scope, schedule, costs, and contracts to fall out of alignment, while percentage-complete reports may conceal that actual earned value is below plan.
predictive-control./opening/1: This lesson follows a project that implements an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show that one decision always affects multiple parties. You will encounter the same group of stakeholders in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
predictive-control./opening/2: Control is not about preventing change; it is about making change transparent and ensuring that decisions are made by the appropriate authority. Data such as EV, AC, defects, accepted deliverables, and forecasts help the Project Manager understand variances, predict outcomes, and select an appropriate corrective action. As you read, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the reasoning sequence: understand the context, verify the information, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plans or documents.

predictive-control./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The integrated change control section answers this question: What must the Project Manager understand before asking the team to act? Each point below is connected to the business objective, project constraints, affected parties, and evidence that must be retained.
predictive-control./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Every change request must be documented, analyzed for impact, and submitted to the designated approval authority; it must not be implemented merely because the sponsor requested it verbally. For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand integrated change control instead of merely memorizing tool names.
predictive-control./sections/1/paragraphs/2: After approval, update the plans, baselines, and related documents, communicate the decision, and track its implementation. For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand integrated change control instead of merely memorizing tool names.
predictive-control./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Corrective action brings future performance back in line with the plan; preventive action reduces the probability of a variance; defect repair corrects a nonconforming product. For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand integrated change control instead of merely memorizing tool names.

If you cannot clearly state who does what, why they do it, and how the result is evaluated, your knowledge remains at the memorization level. In PMP questions, prioritize choices that fit the context and role and that address the root cause.

predictive-control./sections/2/paragraphs/1: PV is the planned value, EV is the value of completed work, and AC is the actual cost; CV = EV−AC, and SV = EV−PV. For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand earned value management instead of merely memorizing tool names.
predictive-control./sections/2/paragraphs/2: CPI = EV/AC and SPI = EV/PV; a value below 1 is unfavorable. Select the EAC formula according to the underlying assumption: BAC/CPI or AC + (BAC−EV). For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand earned value management instead of merely memorizing tool names.

For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand acceptance and closure instead of merely memorizing tool names.
predictive-control./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Closing a project includes confirming completion, transitioning the result, completing contract activities, updating lessons learned, archiving records, and releasing resources. For the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documents that must be updated. This approach helps you understand acceptance and closure instead of merely memorizing tool names.
predictive-control./sections/3/paragraphs/3: Even if a project is terminated early, appropriate closure must still be performed, the reasons must be documented, and knowledge must be preserved. predictive-control./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach for Real-World Work
predictive-control./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Document the request or variance using factual information, and do not implement it immediately. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
predictive-control./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Analyze the integrated impact and the available response options. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
predictive-control./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Submit the decision to the appropriate change authority. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
predictive-control./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: After approval, update the baselines, plans, contracts, and communications. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
predictive-control./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Measure implementation results, obtain acceptance of the deliverable, and complete the transition and closure with supporting evidence. Clearly identify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it produces greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
predictive-control./sections/5/title: A Decision-Making Framework for Selecting Actions
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In scenario-based questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean governance can be ignored. Determine the event's current status, its impact, who has decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If the sponsor makes a verbal request, document and analyze it before implementation. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If CPI or SPI is unfavorable, identify the cause and prepare a forecast before selecting a corrective action. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If a deliverable meets quality requirements but has not been accepted by the customer, perform Validate Scope. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If a change is approved, update all relevant documents and stakeholders. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/5: Before closing a project, verify acceptance, transition, contracts, lessons learned, and the release of resources. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
predictive-control./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong choice usually respects people, process, and business objectives at the same time. PMP therefore evaluates contextual judgment more than the ability to memorize lists of processes.
predictive-control./comparison/0/useWhen: Use integrated change control, EVM, formal acceptance, and a closure checklist.
predictive-control./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration components of the project implementing an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, the sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, are baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through the designated authority.
predictive-control./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
predictive-control./comparison/1/useWhen: The backlog can change flexibly within the product goal boundaries, while release or budget governance is still maintained.
predictive-control./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on real feedback.
predictive-control./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as unrestricted work; objectives, roles, Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
predictive-control./comparison/2/useWhen: The backlog manages features, while changes to integration milestones or contracts go through shared governance.
predictive-control./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested through iterations; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
predictive-control./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are joined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
predictive-control./caseStudy/context: The sponsor requests an additional dashboard before go-live. The team considers it a small request, but the vendor must change the API, UAT must be rerun, and the migration rehearsal could lose its scheduled slot. At the same time, the report states that the project is 70% complete, although many deliverables have not yet been accepted.
predictive-control./caseStudy/steps/0: The Project Manager documents the change request and does not allow the team to start coding immediately.
predictive-control./caseStudy/steps/1: The team analyzes the impacts on the API, regression testing, rehearsal schedule, and vendor costs.
predictive-control./caseStudy/steps/2: The change authority decides to defer the dashboard to a later release to protect the go-live date.
predictive-control./caseStudy/steps/3: The backlog, release notes, and stakeholder expectations are updated.
predictive-control./caseStudy/steps/4: The remaining deliverables undergo quality checks and are then submitted for formal acceptance.
predictive-control./caseStudy/outcome: The change is not rejected based on subjective judgment; the decision is based on its impact. The baseline remains reliable, the team understands the rationale behind the priority, and the project closes only after acceptance and transition are complete.
predictive-control./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Rejecting every change to protect the baseline.

predictive-control./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Closing the project when the team finishes coding.
predictive-control./commonMistakes/2/reason: Technical completion does not mean the outcome has been accepted and transitioned.

predictive-control./examMindset/4: Select an action appropriate to the point in time addressed by the question: the first action is usually to assess or clarify, while the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.

agile-foundations./summary: Understand Agile as a mindset for managing uncertainty through short feedback loops, not as a fixed set of ceremonies.
agile-foundations./objectives/0: Distinguish among iterative, incremental, Agile, and Hybrid approaches.
agile-foundations./objectives/1: Understand Scrum accountabilities, events, artifacts, and commitments.
agile-foundations./objectives/2: Develop a value-driven product vision, roadmap, backlog, and release strategy.
agile-foundations./opening/0: The team says it is Agile because it holds a daily meeting and uses a task board, yet it still receives requirements in large batches, does not review the product with users, and has no authority to adjust how it works. If ceremonies exist but feedback does not change the product, they are merely a formality.
agile-foundations./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as its recurring scenario. The project involves a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show that one decision always affects multiple parties. You will encounter the same participants again in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
agile-foundations./opening/2: Agile is the ability to deliver value in short cycles, make work transparent, obtain feedback, and adapt. Scrum is a specific framework with accountabilities, events, and artifacts; it does not replace product thinking, quality engineering, or necessary governance. As you read, do not try to turn every situation into a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.
