agile-foundations./sections/4/title: A step-by-step approach to real work
agile-foundations./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Clarify the product vision, users, and outcome. Specify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-foundations./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Establish an empowered Product Owner who can prioritize and a cross-functional team. Specify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-foundations./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Order the Product Backlog by value, risk, and learning. Specify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-foundations./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Deliver a small increment that meets the Definition of Done, then review it with actual stakeholders. Specify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-foundations./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Use the retrospective to improve ways of working and update the backlog based on feedback. Specify the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-foundations./sections/5/title: A thinking framework for choosing an action
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the status of the event, its impact, who has decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If the Product Owner lacks prioritization authority, clarify governance before asking for a better backlog. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If the Sprint Review is only a demonstration, invite stakeholders who can provide feedback and make decisions. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If an increment is not Done, do not present it as completed value. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If a requirement is large, divide it by user outcome rather than by technical layer. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If the team encounters a blocker, the Scrum Master/servant leader helps remove it and develops the team's ability to resolve similar issues independently. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-foundations./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong option usually respects people, process, and business objectives at the same time. Therefore, PMP assesses judgment in context more than the ability to memorize process lists.
agile-foundations./comparison/0/useWhen: Less suitable for work that requires discovery; may be used for stable constraints and clearly defined contracts.
agile-foundations./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration work for the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, is baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through defined authorities.
agile-foundations./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and addressed through change control.
agile-foundations./comparison/1/useWhen: Use iterations, a backlog, and feedback when needs or solutions remain uncertain.
agile-foundations./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed through small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on actual feedback.
agile-foundations./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without discipline; goals, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
agile-foundations./comparison/2/useWhen: Maintain shared release governance while allowing the product team to explore the experience through Sprints.
agile-foundations./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the shared plan, while the user experience is tested through iterations; the two workstreams have clear integration points.
agile-foundations./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are integrated only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/context: The order platform interface team receives a list of 80 features and is told to “run Scrum.” The Product Owner has not been given prioritization authority, and users see the product only at the end of the quarter, so the Sprint Review does not result in decisions.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/steps/0: The sponsor gives the Product Owner from the sales department authority to prioritize the backlog.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/steps/1: The team writes a Product Goal linked to order processing time.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/steps/2: The backlog is divided into small user outcomes rather than large technical modules.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/steps/3: Actual sales staff participate in the Sprint Review and try the increment.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/steps/4: The retrospective selects one workflow improvement with an owner for the next Sprint.
agile-foundations./caseStudy/outcome: Scrum events create genuine transparency, inspection, and adaptation. The team does more than complete tasks: it learns which workflows reduce processing time and adjusts investments based on data.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Having ceremonies makes a team Agile.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/0/better: Assess the ability to deliver value and adapt.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/0/reason: Formalities do not create feedback or empowerment.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/1/wrong: The Product Owner is a requirements secretary.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/1/better: Empower the Product Owner to optimize value and the backlog.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/1/reason: Without authority, the backlog is merely a queue of requests.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Change work at any time during the Sprint.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/2/better: Protect the Sprint Goal and negotiate scope appropriately.
agile-foundations./commonMistakes/2/reason: Adaptation still requires focus and transparency.
agile-foundations./examMindset/0: Deliver small increments of value and learn from feedback.
agile-foundations./examMindset/1: Make issues transparent to enable inspection and adaptation.
agile-foundations./examMindset/2: Do not freeze the backlog merely to protect the old plan.
agile-foundations./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting relevant people before taking forceful action, except when safety, legal, or ethical concerns require immediate protection.
agile-foundations./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time addressed by the question: the first action is often to assess or clarify; the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
agile-foundations./recap/0: The core of Agile: Foundations is turning knowledge into decisions that have context, an owner, and an observable result.
agile-foundations./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Product vision, Roadmap, Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, and Increment, but documentation provides value only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
agile-foundations./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
agile-foundations./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for choosing answers reflexively.
agile-foundations./selfCheck/0: A stakeholder wants to add urgent work during a Sprint. Who decides, and what must be protected first?
agile-foundations./selfCheck/1: Can you explain this topic to a new colleague using an example without rereading the definition?
agile-foundations./selfCheck/2: Can you identify the information to gather, the people to consult, and the artifact to update in the sample project case?
agile-foundations./selfCheck/3: If the approach changes from Predictive to Agile, where will the sequence of actions and decision-making authority change?

agile-foundations./practice/title: Turn a feature list into a Product Backlog
agile-foundations./practice/context: The list of 80 features is unordered; the goal is to reduce order entry time and enable reviews with 10 employees every two weeks.
agile-foundations./practice/learnerRole: You are the Product Owner working with the Project Manager and Scrum Team.
agile-foundations./practice/inputs/0: Product outcome: reduce processing time by 30%.
agile-foundations./practice/inputs/2: One API dependency that requires six weeks.
agile-foundations./practice/inputs/3: The team can deliver one small increment per Sprint.
agile-foundations./practice/tasks/0: Write a Product Goal and two outcome metrics.
agile-foundations./practice/tasks/2: Select backlog items for the first two Sprints based on value, risk, and learning.
agile-foundations./practice/tasks/3: Define the feedback to obtain during the Sprint Review.
agile-foundations./practice/deliverable: A product canvas containing the goal, ordered backlog, Sprint hypotheses, and review questions.
agile-foundations./practice/guidance/0: Express the goal as a behavioral change or result.
agile-foundations./practice/guidance/1: Prioritize work that tests major assumptions.
agile-foundations./practice/guidance/2: Do not divide work solely into frontend/backend.
agile-foundations./practice/guidance/3: Review questions must lead to backlog decisions.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/0/evidence: The goal clearly identifies the users and the desired change.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/0/importance: Keeps the team focused on value.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/1/evidence: The ordering is based on value, risk, dependency, and learning.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/1/importance: Makes the backlog a decision-making tool.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Each Sprint produces something users can try.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/3/evidence: Questions are defined, and decisions follow the feedback.
agile-foundations./practice/rubric/3/importance: Turns the review into inspection and adaptation.
agile-foundations./practice/sampleAnswer/0: Product Goal: sales staff create an accurate order in under five minutes.
agile-foundations./practice/sampleAnswer/1: Sprint 1 tests customer search and draft creation; Sprint 2 checks pricing and submits the order for approval.
agile-foundations./practice/sampleAnswer/2: The API dependency is investigated with an early spike to reduce risk.
agile-foundations./practice/sampleAnswer/3: The review asks which steps still require repeated data entry and prioritizes changes based on observed time.
agile-foundations./practice/commonMistakes/0: Order the backlog according to the seniority of the requester.
agile-foundations./practice/commonMistakes/2: Put all 80 features into a single committed release.
agile-foundations./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Which item generates the most learning?
agile-foundations./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: What authority does the Product Owner need for the backlog to provide real value?

agile-delivery./summary: Manage delivery through flow, feedback, built-in quality, and metrics that support decision-making rather than individual evaluation.
agile-delivery./objectives/0: Use Kanban, WIP limits, cycle time, and cumulative flow to improve flow.
agile-delivery./objectives/1: Estimate and forecast based on velocity/throughput without turning them into firm commitments.
agile-delivery./objectives/2: Manage technical debt, quality, risk, and stakeholder feedback within the Agile cadence.
agile-delivery./opening/0: After several Sprints, velocity increases, but defects and release wait times also rise. The team may be optimizing the number of completed points rather than flow and outcomes; technical debt is deferred, making each change progressively slower.
agile-delivery./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as a continuing scenario. It involves a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show how one decision can affect many parties. You will encounter the same group of people in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
agile-delivery./opening/2: Agile delivery manages the flow of value from an idea to a usable increment. Estimation, velocity, burn charts, and cycle time are only signals; integrated quality, the Definition of Done, WIP limits, release readiness, and user feedback indicate whether the value delivery system is healthy. As you read, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

agile-delivery./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach for Real-World Work
agile-delivery./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Map the flow from backlog to production and identify the longest wait time. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-delivery./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Clarify the Definition of Ready/Done and the integrated quality required in every increment. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-delivery./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Limit WIP and address blockers and dependencies early. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-delivery./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Create a release forecast using actual data and provide stakeholders with transparent updates. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-delivery./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Use reviews, retrospectives, and outcome metrics to select the next small improvement. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected result; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
agile-delivery./sections/5/title: A Framework for Choosing an Action
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the event's status, its impact, the decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If velocity increases but outcomes remain unchanged, examine quality and flow before asking the team to work faster. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not simply transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If a story is waiting, address the blocker or dependency and limit WIP. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not simply transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If Done does not include integration, revise the Definition of Done instead of repeatedly creating a hardening phase. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not simply transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If the forecast is uncertain, use a range and historical data instead of making a false commitment. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not simply transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If a release has compliance requirements, integrate reviewers and evidence into the flow instead of checking them at the end. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not simply transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
agile-delivery./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong choice usually respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. Therefore, PMP evaluates judgment in context more than the ability to memorize process lists.
agile-delivery./comparison/0/useWhen: Use milestones and quality gates when a release requires fixed approvals.
agile-delivery./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration components of the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, are baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through defined authorities.
agile-delivery./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
agile-delivery./comparison/1/useWhen: Use flow metrics, iterations, and continuous integration to reduce batch size.
agile-delivery./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on actual feedback.
agile-delivery./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without structure; objectives, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
agile-delivery./comparison/2/useWhen: The team delivers increments in Sprints, but releases pass through operational/compliance gates that have been integrated early.
agile-delivery./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two streams have clearly defined integration points.
agile-delivery./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects and integrate only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/context: The order platform team completes many stories, but every release requires three weeks of hardening. Stories are counted as Done before integration testing, the vendor API changes frequently, and operations learns the release contents only shortly before the release date.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/steps/0: The team measures cycle time and finds that most of it is spent waiting for integration testing.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/steps/1: The Definition of Done is updated to include automated regression and API contract testing.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/steps/2: The WIP limit reduces the number of stories open at the same time.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/steps/3: Operations participates in refinement and reviews for changes that affect the runbook.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/steps/4: After three Sprints, the release forecast becomes more stable even though velocity does not increase significantly.
agile-delivery./caseStudy/outcome: The team shifts from optimizing the number of stories completed to optimizing value flow and quality. Hardening is reduced, stakeholders receive more reliable forecasts, and feedback is incorporated into the backlog earlier.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/0/better: Use metrics to improve the team's own flow.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/0/reason: A story point is not a standardized unit of productivity.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Increasing WIP to keep everyone busy.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/1/better: Finish current work before starting more.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/1/reason: High utilization can increase lead time.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Leaving quality until the final Sprint.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/2/better: Build quality into the Definition of Done.
agile-delivery./commonMistakes/2/reason: A large batch of defects delays feedback and increases release risk.
agile-delivery./examMindset/0: Optimize the flow system; do not force every individual to remain 100% busy.
agile-delivery./examMindset/1: Use data for learning and forecasting, not punishment.
agile-delivery./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting relevant people before taking forceful action, except in safety, legal, or ethical situations that require immediate protection.
agile-delivery./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time described by the question: the first action is usually to assess or clarify; a final solution comes only after sufficient facts and authority are available.
agile-delivery./recap/0: The core of Agile: Delivery & Flow is turning knowledge into decisions with context, an owner, and observable results.
agile-delivery./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Kanban board, Cumulative flow diagram, Burnup/Burndown, Definition of Done, and Impediment log, but documentation is valuable only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
agile-delivery./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
agile-delivery./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for reflexively selecting an answer.
agile-delivery./selfCheck/0: Velocity decreases after the team strengthens the Definition of Done. Does this necessarily indicate poor performance, or should you reconsider what the metric means?
agile-delivery./selfCheck/1: Can you explain this topic to a new colleague using an example without rereading the definition?
agile-delivery./selfCheck/2: Can you identify the facts to collect, the people to consult, and the artifact to update in the sample project case?
agile-delivery./selfCheck/3: If the approach changes from Predictive to Agile, where will the sequence of actions and decision-making authority change?

agile-delivery./practice/context: Velocity has increased by 20%, but cycle time has doubled; 12 stories are waiting for integration testing, and each release requires three weeks of hardening.
agile-delivery./practice/learnerRole: You are the Agile Project Manager supporting the Scrum Team and operations.
agile-delivery./practice/inputs/0: The Definition of Done requires only a code review.
agile-delivery./practice/inputs/2: The vendor provides an unstable sandbox.
agile-delivery./practice/tasks/0: Identify the bottleneck and the metrics to track.
agile-delivery./practice/tasks/1: Propose changes to the Definition of Done.
agile-delivery./practice/tasks/2: Design two flow improvements, each with an owner.
agile-delivery./practice/tasks/3: Describe how to forecast the release transparently.
agile-delivery./practice/deliverable: A flow improvement brief covering the current flow, bottleneck, new policy, experiment, and metrics.
agile-delivery./practice/guidance/0: Examine wait time, not just work time.
agile-delivery./practice/guidance/1: Do not use velocity as the improvement target.
agile-delivery./practice/guidance/2: Each experiment must be small enough to measure after a few Sprints.
agile-delivery./practice/guidance/3: Bring the dependency owner into the appropriate work cadence.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/0/evidence: Identifies integration testing and the sandbox dependency.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/0/importance: Improving the wrong constraint will not reduce lead time.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/1/evidence: Includes integration/regression and the necessary evidence.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/1/importance: Prevents unfinished work from being deferred until the end.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Includes an owner, a timeframe, and cycle time/WIP/defect metrics.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/2/importance: A retrospective must produce observable change.
agile-delivery./practice/rubric/3/importance: Helps stakeholders make decisions based on genuine uncertainty.
agile-delivery./practice/sampleAnswer/0: The bottleneck is the integration queue; measure WIP age and cycle time.
agile-delivery./practice/sampleAnswer/1: Done includes API contract testing, regression testing, and runbook impact assessment.
agile-delivery./practice/sampleAnswer/2: Cross-train a developer to support testing; create a sandbox stub with the vendor owner.
agile-delivery./practice/sampleAnswer/3: Base the forecast on throughput over three Sprints and provide a range instead of a guaranteed date.
agile-delivery./practice/commonMistakes/0: Asking the tester to work overtime.
agile-delivery./practice/commonMistakes/1: Increasing story points to reflect the difficulty.
agile-delivery./practice/commonMistakes/2: Adding another status meeting without changing the flow.
agile-delivery./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Which metric is encouraging the wrong behavior?
agile-delivery./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: Which improvement will make the team least dependent in the long term?

financial./summary: Evaluate projects from an investment perspective: life-cycle costs, cash flow, benefits, forecasts, and value-based decisions.
financial./objectives/0: Understand ROI, NPV, IRR, payback period, and benefit-cost ratio.
financial./objectives/1: Develop and control the budget, forecast, reserves, and cash flow.
financial./objectives/2: Link financial metrics to the business case and benefits realization.
financial./opening/0: Financial decisions in projects involve more than comparing the lowest prices. An option with a low initial cost may create substantial operating costs, risks, or opportunity costs; an estimate with false precision may cause the sponsor to misunderstand the level of uncertainty.
financial./opening/1: This lesson uses a multi-department order management platform implementation as its recurring scenario. The project involves a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The example is familiar enough to keep newcomers from getting lost in technical details, yet complex enough to show how a decision always affects multiple parties. You will encounter the same stakeholders in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, information, and type of decision required.
financial./opening/2: Financial management connects the business case, funding, estimates, budget, reserves, cash flow, and benefits. The Project Manager must understand how each figure was produced, which assumptions it uses, which baseline or reserve it belongs to, and which business decisions depend on it. As you read, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the reasoning sequence: understand the context, verify the information, consult the right people, assess the impact, select an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or document.
financial./sections/0/title: What will this lesson enable you to do?
financial./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Understand ROI, NPV, IRR, payback period, and benefit-cost ratio. For this Project Finance lesson, you should be able to explain each concept, recognize signals in a situation, and select the next action. Distinguish outputs, such as plans or registers, from outcomes, such as alignment, predictability, or protected value.

financial./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Investment appraisal answers this question: What must the Project Manager understand before asking the team to act? Each point below is connected to the business objective, project constraints, affected stakeholders, and evidence that must be retained.
financial./sections/1/paragraphs/1: NPV discounts future cash flows to their present value; all else being equal, a higher NPV is generally more attractive. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of investment appraisal instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/1/paragraphs/2: IRR is the discount rate at which NPV equals 0; the payback period is easy to understand but may disregard the time value of money and benefits received after the payback point. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of investment appraisal instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/1/paragraphs/3: ROI = (benefits − costs)/costs; agree on the measurement period, cost categories, and assumptions before making comparisons. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of investment appraisal instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/1/paragraphs/4: Explain this section using a situation from your own experience. If you cannot clearly state who does what, why they do it, and how the result is evaluated, your knowledge remains at the recall level. In PMP questions, favor the option that fits the context and role and addresses the underlying cause.
financial./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Cost planning and reserves answer this question: What must the Project Manager understand before asking the team to act? Each point below is connected to the business objective, project constraints, affected stakeholders, and evidence that must be retained.
financial./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Direct costs are directly attributable to the project; indirect costs are allocated; fixed and variable costs describe how costs change with output. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of cost planning and reserves instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Contingency reserve addresses identified risks and is included in the cost baseline; management reserve is controlled by management and remains outside the baseline. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of cost planning and reserves instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Funding limit reconciliation adjusts the spending schedule to align with the organization's funding limits and cash flow. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of cost planning and reserves instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/2/paragraphs/4: Explain this section using a situation from your own experience. In PMP questions, favor the option that fits the context and role and addresses the underlying cause.
financial./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Value and benefits answer this question: What must the Project Manager understand before asking the team to act? Each point below is connected to the business objective, project constraints, affected stakeholders, and evidence that must be retained.
financial./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Track outcomes and benefits after handover; completing the scope does not mean that value has been realized. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of value and benefits instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When business assumptions change, update the business case and decide whether to continue, change direction, or stop. In the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor, turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify the decision authority, and specify which evidence or documents must be updated. This approach builds an understanding of value and benefits instead of merely memorizing tool names.
financial./sections/3/paragraphs/3: Sunk costs should not influence future decisions; consider the remaining costs, remaining benefits, and current risks. This approach builds an understanding of value and benefits instead of merely memorizing tool names.

In PMP questions, favor the option that fits the context and role and addresses the underlying cause.
financial./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach to Real-World Work
financial./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Define the decision horizon, cash flows, and business outcomes. Clearly record the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
financial./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Estimate costs using an appropriate method, and document the assumptions and range. Clearly record the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
financial./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Analyze total costs, benefits, NPV/ROI, or payback when appropriate. Clearly record the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
financial./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Establish the cost baseline, contingency, and funding requirements. Clearly record the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
financial./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Track actuals, forecasts, and benefits; update them when assumptions change. Clearly record the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates greater understanding, a decision, or evidence.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean immediate implementation, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the event's status and impact, the decision authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If an estimate provides only one figure, ask for its range, confidence level, and assumptions. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If an option has a low initial cost, calculate its total cost and risk exposure. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If a risk has been identified, consider using contingency reserve; do not use management reserve arbitrarily. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If the business case changes, evaluate whether to continue, adjust, or stop the investment. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If the benefits have no owner, financial success cannot yet be declared. Then determine whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
financial./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong option usually respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. The PMP exam therefore evaluates contextual judgment more than the ability to memorize process lists.
financial./comparison/0/useWhen: Plan the budget and funding by phase or work package when the scope is stable.
financial./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration work for the multi-department order management platform project involving a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor is baselined, with dependencies and changes managed through defined authorities.
financial./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
financial./comparison/1/useWhen: Fund by increment or capacity, and prioritize the backlog by value and cost of delay.
financial./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on actual feedback.
financial./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without discipline; objectives, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
financial./comparison/2/useWhen: Maintain shared business case and funding governance while adjusting feature investment based on feedback.
financial./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
financial./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
financial./caseStudy/context: Two vendors propose order management platforms: Option A costs 20% less but has high transaction fees; Option B requires a larger initial investment but reduces operating costs and supports scaling. The sponsor wants to consider only the first-year contract price.
financial./caseStudy/steps/0: The team expands the analysis from purchase price to three-year total cost.
financial./caseStudy/steps/1: Transaction volume is modeled using three scenarios.
financial./caseStudy/steps/2: The known migration risk has a dedicated contingency; management reserve is not included in the baseline.
financial./caseStudy/steps/3: The sponsor compares NPV, payback, and strategic fit instead of relying on a single figure.
financial./caseStudy/steps/4: The benefits owner takes responsibility for the post-go-live cost-per-order metric.
financial./caseStudy/outcome: The vendor decision reflects the entire life cycle and the level of uncertainty. The budget has a sound basis, each reserve serves the correct purpose, and the sponsor knows which assumptions must be monitored.
financial./commonMistakes/0/better: Compare total cost, value, and risk.
financial./commonMistakes/0/reason: Life-cycle costs may reverse the initial decision.
financial./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Add every buffer to the baseline.
financial./commonMistakes/1/better: Separate contingency reserve from management reserve.
financial./commonMistakes/1/reason: Each reserve must correspond to the appropriate risk type and authority.
financial./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Treat sunk costs as a reason to continue.
financial./commonMistakes/2/better: Evaluate the remaining value and future costs.
financial./commonMistakes/2/reason: Costs already incurred do not make a future option better.
financial./examMindset/0: Do not continue solely because substantial funds have already been spent.
financial./examMindset/1: Clearly state the assumptions and horizon for every figure.
financial./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting the relevant stakeholders before taking strong measures, except when immediate action is required to protect safety, legal compliance, or ethics.
financial./examMindset/4: Select an action appropriate to the point in time described by the question: the first action is usually to assess or clarify; the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.

financial./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for selecting answers reflexively.

financial./practice/context: Vendor A costs VND 4 billion plus VND 2.000 per order; Vendor B costs VND 5 billion plus VND 800 per order. The forecast is 800.000 orders per year for three years.
financial./practice/learnerRole: You are the Project Manager preparing a financial recommendation.
financial./practice/inputs/0: The initial implementation costs of the two vendors.
financial./practice/inputs/1: The baseline volume and a possible variation of ±25%.
financial./practice/inputs/2: A migration risk of VND 300 million with a 40% probability.
financial./practice/inputs/3: The target outcome of reducing manual processing costs.
financial./practice/tasks/0: Calculate the total transaction cost for the baseline scenario.
financial./practice/tasks/1: State the expected monetary value of the migration risk.
financial./practice/tasks/2: Compare the three-year total cost and nonfinancial factors.
financial./practice/tasks/3: Recommend a reserve and decision criteria.
financial./practice/deliverable: A financial option paper covering scenarios, assumptions, total cost, risk, value, and a recommendation.
financial./practice/guidance/0: Separate facts from the volume assumptions.
financial./practice/guidance/1: Do not mechanically add expected value to every figure.
financial./practice/guidance/2: Include scalability and switching costs in the trade-off.
financial./practice/guidance/3: Describe the sensitivity to changes in volume.
financial./practice/rubric/0/evidence: Includes the formula, units, and three-year horizon.
financial./practice/rubric/0/importance: The sponsor must be able to verify the assumptions.
financial./practice/rubric/1/evidence: The migration EMV is VND 120 million and is presented in the correct context.
financial./practice/rubric/1/importance: Supports the design of an appropriate contingency.
financial./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Includes total cost, value, risk, and strategic fit.
financial./practice/rubric/2/importance: The lowest price is not necessarily the best option.
financial./practice/rubric/3/evidence: States the volume threshold or assumption that would change the decision.
financial./practice/rubric/3/importance: Decisions are more robust when uncertainty is transparent.
financial./practice/sampleAnswer/0: A has transaction fees of VND 4,8 billion over three years; B has VND 1,92 billion, excluding the initial investment.
financial./practice/sampleAnswer/1: A's baseline total is VND 8,8 billion; B's is VND 6,92 billion, so B is better at this volume.
financial./practice/sampleAnswer/2: The migration EMV of VND 120 million is used as an input to contingency planning and does not obscure other risks.
financial./practice/sampleAnswer/3: Recommend B if the volume and scalability are confirmed; test sensitivity at -25%.
financial./practice/commonMistakes/0: Forgetting to multiply the volume across three years.
financial./practice/commonMistakes/1: Treating EMV as a certain cost.
financial./practice/commonMistakes/2: Failing to document the assumption that would change the recommendation.

quality./summary: Build quality into products and processes, distinguish prevention from inspection, and address root causes.
quality./objectives/0: Distinguish quality, grade, precision, accuracy, tolerance, and control limits.
quality./objectives/1: Develop quality metrics, a quality management plan, and acceptance criteria.
quality./objectives/2: Use tools to analyze causes and variation and prioritize defects.
quality./opening/0: Teams often say, “quality is QA’s responsibility,” and wait until close to delivery to perform inspections. By then, defects have accumulated, acceptance criteria remain ambiguous, and correcting defects costs more than preventing them. Quality must be built into requirements, workflows, and daily decisions.
quality./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as its recurring scenario. The project involves a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The example is familiar enough to keep beginners from getting stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show how one decision always affects multiple parties. You will encounter the same participants in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, available information, and type of decision required.
quality./opening/2: Quality management identifies appropriate standards, converts them into requirements and acceptance criteria, builds processes capable of producing the correct results, and uses data to inspect outputs. Manage Quality improves processes; Control Quality measures deliverables; Validate Scope obtains customer acceptance. As you read, do not try to turn every situation into a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

quality./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Quality is the degree to which requirements are met. A low grade may be acceptable if it matches the design, but low quality is not. In the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments—with a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor—turn this idea into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documentation that must be updated. This approach helps you understand planning for quality instead of merely memorizing tool names.

quality./sections/4/title: A step-by-step approach for real work
quality./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Convert needs and standards into measurable quality requirements. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
quality./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Develop the quality management plan, metrics, methods, and responsibilities. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
quality./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Build prevention, reviews, and testing into the delivery flow. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
quality./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Use data and root cause analysis to examine defects and processes. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
quality./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Control the deliverable, correct nonconformities, and then submit it for acceptance against the agreed criteria. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it creates additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
quality./sections/5/title: A framework for choosing an action
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In scenario questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean abandoning governance. Determine the event’s status, impact, decision authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If a requirement is not measurable, clarify the criteria before proceeding. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions within their authority.
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If a defect recurs, analyze the root cause and process instead of correcting each defect individually. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions within their authority.
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/3: Even if an output passes internal testing, it still requires acceptance from the appropriate stakeholder. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions within their authority.
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If someone wants to reduce testing to preserve the schedule, assess the risk and governance requirements instead of concealing the issue. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions within their authority.
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/5: If an average metric looks good but users complain, examine the distribution and real-world scenarios. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value; do not merely transfer responsibility when the Project Manager still has actions within their authority.
quality./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong option usually respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. Therefore, PMP evaluates judgment in context more than the ability to memorize process lists.
quality./comparison/0/useWhen: Use a quality plan, audits, a test plan, and quality gates for each deliverable.
quality./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration components of the order management platform implementation project across multiple departments—with a business sponsor, sales and operations groups, an internal development team, and an external vendor—are baselined, while dependencies and changes are managed through the designated authority.
quality./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and addressed through change control.
quality./comparison/1/useWhen: Use acceptance criteria, the Definition of Done, automated testing, and feedback in every increment.
quality./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on real feedback.
quality./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without structure; objectives, roles, the Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
quality./comparison/2/useWhen: Maintain common mandatory standards while testing continuously during iterations before the release gate.
quality./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
quality./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
quality./caseStudy/context: The order platform functions correctly, but many transactions take more than ten seconds. The requirement says only that “the system is fast,” QA has no threshold, and the sponsor believes the product is not ready for acceptance.
quality./caseStudy/steps/0: The Project Manager brings the sponsor, users, and technical team together to define a target latency.
quality./caseStudy/steps/1: The metric is segmented by transaction type and percentile instead of using one overall average.
quality./caseStudy/steps/2: The team adds performance testing to the pipeline early.
quality./caseStudy/steps/3: Root cause analysis reveals that the customer search query has no index.
quality./caseStudy/steps/4: After the correction, Control Quality confirms that the threshold is met, and the sponsor then performs Validate Scope.
quality./caseStudy/outcome: The concept of “fast” becomes a testable criterion. The team detects defects early, distinguishes quality control from acceptance, and avoids subjective arguments.
quality./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Waiting until the end of the project to test.
quality./commonMistakes/0/better: Build quality into planning and delivery.
quality./commonMistakes/0/reason: Early prevention costs less than correcting a batch of defects.
quality./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Confusing Control Quality with Validate Scope.
quality./commonMistakes/1/better: Separate verification against standards from formal acceptance.
quality./commonMistakes/1/reason: The two activities have different objectives and participants.
quality./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Setting a “zero defect” objective without context.
quality./commonMistakes/2/better: Use standards appropriate to risk and value.
quality./commonMistakes/2/reason: Quality is the degree of conformance to requirements, not an absolute slogan.
quality./examMindset/0: Preventing defects at the source is better than inspecting at the end.
quality./examMindset/1: Correct systemic causes instead of blaming individuals.
quality./examMindset/2: Acceptance and quality control are two different steps.
quality./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting stakeholders before taking forceful action, except when immediate action is required to protect safety or address legal or ethical concerns.
quality./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time addressed by the question: the first action is usually to assess or clarify; the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
quality./recap/0: The core of Quality is turning knowledge into decisions with context, an owner, and observable results.
quality./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Quality management plan, Quality metrics, Test and evaluation documents, and Quality reports, but documentation has value only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
quality./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
quality./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for selecting answers reflexively.
quality./selfCheck/0: The product has passed internal testing, but the customer has not accepted it. What is the status of the deliverable?
quality./selfCheck/1: Can you explain this topic to a new colleague using an example without rereading the definition?
quality./selfCheck/2: Can you identify the information to collect, the people to consult, and the artifact to update in the sample project case?

quality./practice/title: Turn “a fast system” into a quality plan
quality./practice/context: Users complain that transactions are slow; the requirement contains only the phrase “fast response,” and the release is six weeks away.
quality./practice/learnerRole: You are the Project Manager leading quality planning.
quality./practice/inputs/0: Three transaction types have different loads.
quality./practice/inputs/1: The sponsor is concerned about the peak-hour experience.
quality./practice/inputs/2: The team has a pipeline, but it does not yet run performance tests.
quality./practice/inputs/3: Operations retains production metrics from the legacy system.
quality./practice/tasks/1: Select the metrics, sampling approach, and responsible person.
quality./practice/tasks/2: Build prevention and testing into the delivery flow.
quality./practice/deliverable: A quality card containing the requirement, metric, test method, threshold, owner, and acceptance flow.
quality./practice/guidance/1: Use legacy system data as a reference baseline.
quality./practice/guidance/2: Test under a load representative of peak hours.
quality./practice/guidance/3: Clearly identify who performs technical verification and who grants business acceptance.
quality./practice/rubric/0/evidence: Includes the transaction, percentile, threshold, and condition.
quality./practice/rubric/1/evidence: Includes latency, error rate, and a representative load.
quality./practice/rubric/1/importance: A single metric can conceal defects.
quality./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Performance testing runs before release and has an owner.
quality./practice/rubric/3/evidence: QA confirms the standards; the sponsor/user grants acceptance.
quality./practice/sampleAnswer/0: 95% of order creation operations complete in under two seconds with 500 concurrent users; the error rate is below 0.5%.
quality./practice/sampleAnswer/1: The pipeline runs a smoke test on every build and a full load test weekly.
quality./practice/sampleAnswer/2: The performance owner investigates regression; the Product Owner confirms the experience during UAT.
quality./practice/sampleAnswer/3: Control Quality produces a report; the sponsor signs the acceptance when the criteria are met.
quality./practice/commonMistakes/0: Setting a threshold without a load condition.
quality./practice/commonMistakes/2: Expecting the sponsor to interpret technical logs to grant acceptance.
quality./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Which criterion directly reflects the user experience?
quality./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: Are you preventing defects or merely detecting them late?

risk./summary: Proactively manage uncertainty at both the individual risk and overall project risk levels; turn threats and opportunities into actions with assigned owners.
risk./objectives/0: Identify, qualitatively and quantitatively analyze, and prioritize risks.
risk./objectives/1: Select appropriate responses for threats, opportunities, and overall risk.
risk./objectives/2: Monitor triggers, residual and secondary risks, and response effectiveness.
risk./opening/0: A risk register can easily become a list of “possible delays and possible defects” with no owner, trigger, or response. When an event occurs, the team calls it a surprise even though it was previously mentioned. Risk management creates value when it turns uncertainty into choices and actions before an emergency arises.
risk./opening/1: This lesson uses a cross-functional order management platform implementation as its running scenario, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get lost in technical details, yet complex enough to show how one decision always affects multiple stakeholders. You will encounter the same group of people in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, available information, and type of decision required.
risk./opening/2: A risk is an uncertain event or condition that may have a positive or negative impact. An issue has already occurred. Risk management is a cycle of identifying and analyzing risks, selecting responses, assigning owners, monitoring triggers, implementing responses, and evaluating residual and secondary risks. As you read, do not try to turn every situation into a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of reasoning: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

risk./caseStudy/context: The vendor warns that the sandbox may not be ready before integration testing. The team records “risk: vendor delay – high” but provides no trigger date, alternative option, or person authorized to decide what to do if the risk occurs.
risk./caseStudy/steps/0: The risk is rewritten: because the vendor lacks capacity, the sandbox may be delayed, which would delay integration testing and go-live.
risk./caseStudy/steps/1: The owner is the vendor manager; the trigger is that the sandbox has not passed the smoke test by the specified date.
risk./caseStudy/steps/2: The mitigation response uses a contract test stub and weekly checkpoints.
risk./caseStudy/steps/3: The contingency is to move integration to a backup environment using the budget already allocated.
risk./caseStudy/steps/4: When the trigger occurs, the owner activates the response and updates the residual risk.
risk./caseStudy/outcome: The team no longer waits for a problem before holding an emergency meeting. The risk has a clear description, an early warning signal, an owner, and prepared options; management understands the exposure and required reserve.
risk./commonMistakes/0/better: Describe the cause, event, impact, owner, and trigger.
risk./commonMistakes/0/reason: The high rating does not indicate what action to take.
risk./commonMistakes/1/better: Assign an owner capable of monitoring the risk and taking action.
risk./commonMistakes/1/reason: Ownership must be matched with authority and capability.
risk./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Wait for the trigger before considering a response.
risk./commonMistakes/2/better: Plan the response and contingency in advance.
risk./commonMistakes/2/reason: The value of risk management lies in preparation.
risk./examMindset/0: Act proactively before the trigger occurs.
risk./examMindset/1: Manage opportunities as seriously as threats.
risk./examMindset/2: Escalate when a risk is outside your authority or the project's scope.
risk./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting relevant stakeholders before taking forceful action, except when safety, legal, or ethical concerns require immediate protection.
risk./examMindset/4: Choose an action appropriate to the point in time addressed by the question: the first action is often to assess or clarify, while the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
risk./recap/0: The core of Risk is turning knowledge into decisions that have context, an owner, and observable results.
risk./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Risk management plan, Risk register, Risk report, Contingency reserve, and Issue log, but documentation is valuable only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
risk./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
risk./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for selecting answers reflexively.

risk./practice/title: Write an Actionable Risk Register Entry
risk./practice/context: There is a 40% probability that the vendor's sandbox will be delayed by three weeks; each week of delay adds 150 million in costs and affects the campaign.
risk./practice/learnerRole: You are the Project Manager preparing a risk review with the sponsor and vendor.
risk./practice/inputs/0: The vendor is short two engineers.
risk./practice/inputs/1: A stub can be built at a cost of 120 million.
risk./practice/inputs/2: The backup environment requires sponsor approval.
risk./practice/tasks/0: Write the risk in cause–event–impact format.
risk./practice/tasks/2: Select the response, owner, trigger, and contingency.
risk./practice/deliverable: One complete risk register entry and a brief escalation summary.
risk./practice/guidance/0: Separate the cause from the event.
risk./practice/guidance/1: The trigger must be observable early enough to allow action.
risk./practice/guidance/2: Compare the response cost with the exposure, but do not make the decision based solely on EMV.
risk./practice/guidance/3: Identify any new risk created by the response.
risk./practice/rubric/0/evidence: Includes a specific cause, event, and impact.
risk./practice/rubric/0/importance: Creates shared understanding and enables accurate analysis.
risk./practice/rubric/1/evidence: Specifies a person, signal, and point in time.
risk./practice/rubric/1/importance: Prevents the response from being overlooked.
risk./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Mitigation and contingency are aligned with exposure.
risk./practice/rubric/2/importance: Prepares options before a crisis occurs.
risk./practice/rubric/3/importance: A risk does not disappear simply because a plan exists.
risk./practice/sampleAnswer/0: Because the vendor lacks engineers, the sandbox may be delayed by three weeks, delaying integration and the campaign at a cost of 450 million.
risk./practice/sampleAnswer/1: Mitigate with a stub costing 120 million; the vendor manager is the owner.
risk./practice/sampleAnswer/2: Trigger: the smoke test has not passed by the T-4 week checkpoint; contingency: request the backup environment.
risk./practice/sampleAnswer/3: Secondary risk: the stub may behave differently from the real system; contract testing and validation are required.
risk./practice/commonMistakes/0: Describe a current issue as a future risk.
risk./practice/commonMistakes/1: Provide no trigger date or condition.
risk./practice/commonMistakes/2: Treat acceptance as ignoring the risk without monitoring it.
risk./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Does the trigger provide enough time to act?
risk./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: Does the risk owner truly have the authority to coordinate the response?

procurement./summary: Select the appropriate make-or-buy approach, contract, and governance mechanisms to allocate risk fairly and protect project value.
procurement./objectives/0: Differentiate fixed-price, cost-reimbursable, and time & materials contracts.
procurement./objectives/1: Develop a procurement strategy, source selection criteria, and statement of work.
procurement./objectives/2: Manage contract claims, changes, performance, and closure.
procurement./opening/0: Procurement does not fail only because a vendor delivers late. Ambiguous scope, a contract type that allocates risk incorrectly, selection criteria overly focused on price, and unclear acceptance criteria can create disputes from the moment the contract is signed. The Project Manager must manage both the relationship and the contractual obligations.
procurement./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as its ongoing scenario. The project includes a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external supplier. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to show how one decision can affect multiple parties. You will encounter the same group of people in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, the available information, and the type of decision required.
procurement./opening/2: Procurement management determines what to buy, when to buy it, which contract type to use, how to select a seller, and how to control performance, changes, and claims. A contract is legally binding, so project actions must be coordinated with procurement, legal counsel, and authorized decision-makers. As you read, do not try to reduce every situation to a rigid formula. Focus on the reasoning sequence: understand the context, verify the facts, consult the right people, assess the impact, choose an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

procurement./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A fixed-price contract transfers most cost risk to the seller when the scope is clear; incentives can be linked to performance objectives. In the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external supplier, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documentation that must be updated. This approach helps you understand contract types instead of merely memorizing tool names.
procurement./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A cost-reimbursable contract is appropriate when the scope is uncertain and the buyer assumes more cost risk; all costs must comply with the allowable-cost provisions. In the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external supplier, turn this principle into concrete action: separate facts from assumptions, identify who has decision-making authority, and specify the evidence or documentation that must be updated. This approach helps you understand contract types instead of merely memorizing tool names.
procurement./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Time & Materials is a hybrid contract type. It is flexible but requires a ceiling, defined rates, and close monitoring to avoid open-ended costs. This approach helps you understand control and close procurement instead of merely memorizing tool names.
