procurement./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach for Real-World Work
procurement./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Perform a make-or-buy analysis and clarify the procurement scope. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
procurement./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Assess the level of certainty to select the contract type and incentives. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
procurement./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Prepare source selection criteria and the procurement request, and ensure fair bidder communication. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
procurement./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Use evidence to manage performance, acceptance, changes, payments, and claims. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
procurement./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Close the procurement after obligations, assets, records, and disputes have been addressed. Clearly document the inputs, participants, and expected results; a step is useful only when it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
procurement./sections/5/title: A Framework for Selecting an Action
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean governance can be ignored. Determine the event's current status, its impact, who has decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If the scope is ambiguous, reduce uncertainty or select an appropriate contract type before forcing a fixed-price arrangement. Then check whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If seller performance is poor, compare it against the contract and performance data before reaching a conclusion. Then check whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/3: If a change is required, use the contract change mechanism and the appropriate authority. Then check whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/4: If there is a claim, preserve the evidence and coordinate with procurement and legal counsel. Then check whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/5: When selecting a vendor, use criteria disclosed in advance and avoid conflicts of interest. Then check whether the action will produce better data, restore collaboration, or protect value; do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
procurement./sections/5/paragraphs/6: A strong choice usually respects people, processes, and business objectives at the same time. Therefore, PMP assesses judgment in context more than the ability to memorize lists of processes.
procurement./comparison/0/useWhen: Fixed price is appropriate for a clear scope; cost reimbursable or T&M requires controls when uncertainty is high.
procurement./comparison/0/projectExample: The infrastructure and mandatory integration work for the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external supplier, is baselined, with dependencies managed and changes processed through the designated authority.
procurement./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable; new information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
procurement./comparison/1/useWhen: Purchase capacity or outcomes by increment, while still defining boundaries and acceptance criteria.
procurement./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed in small increments, reviewed frequently, and the backlog is adjusted based on actual feedback.
procurement./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without controls; objectives, roles, Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
procurement./comparison/2/useWhen: Use a milestone contract for the fixed portion and a change-friendly mechanism for the uncertain backlog.
procurement./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance and data migration milestones follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two workstreams have clearly defined integration points.
procurement./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end; dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
procurement./caseStudy/context: The integration vendor proposes a fixed-price contract even though the API and data are not yet clear. The sponsor prefers it because the budget appears certain, but the seller includes a large contingency and marks many items as “out of scope.”
procurement./caseStudy/steps/0: The team conducts a short discovery effort to reduce API uncertainty before finalizing the price.
procurement./caseStudy/steps/1: The scope is divided into fixed migration work and a variable feature backlog.
procurement./caseStudy/steps/2: The selection criteria include capability, approach, security, support, and total cost.
procurement./caseStudy/steps/3: The contract defines acceptance, the change mechanism, and performance data.
procurement./caseStudy/steps/4: The vendor review uses evidence and a corrective action plan before escalating a claim.
procurement./caseStudy/outcome: Risk is allocated to the party best able to manage it, price is no longer the only criterion, and changes do not become personal disputes. The vendor relationship is managed professionally within contractual boundaries.
procurement./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Fixed price always means less risk for the buyer.
procurement./commonMistakes/0/better: Assess scope clarity and the ability to manage changes.
procurement./commonMistakes/0/reason: The seller may add a premium or submit claims.
procurement./commonMistakes/1/better: Use weighted criteria and total value.
procurement./commonMistakes/1/reason: A low price may conceal capability gaps or lifecycle costs.
procurement./commonMistakes/2/wrong: The PM modifies the contract directly with the vendor.
procurement./commonMistakes/2/better: Follow the procurement and change authority process.
procurement./commonMistakes/2/reason: A contract is a legal document governed by specific authority.
procurement./examMindset/0: Read the contract before taking action.
procurement./examMindset/1: Contract changes must follow the established legal process.
procurement./examMindset/2: Collaborate with the seller while protecting the project's interests.
procurement./examMindset/3: Prioritize understanding the cause and consulting the relevant people before taking strong measures, except when an immediate response is required to address safety, legal, or ethical concerns.
procurement./examMindset/4: Select an action appropriate to the point in time addressed by the question: the first action is usually to assess or clarify, while the final solution comes only after sufficient information and authority are available.
procurement./recap/0: The central principle of Procurement & Contracts is to turn knowledge into decisions with context, an owner, and observable results.
procurement./recap/1: Key artifacts include the Procurement strategy, Procurement SOW, Bid documents, Contract, and Claims administration records, but documentation is valuable only when it supports communication, decision-making, and outcome tracking.
procurement./recap/2: Always connect People, Process, and Business Environment; a change in one domain almost always affects another.
procurement./recap/3: Predictive, Agile, and Hybrid are ways of organizing work, not labels for selecting answers reflexively.

procurement./practice/title: Select a Contract Type and Vendor
procurement./practice/context: The migration is relatively well defined, but the features and API remain subject to change. Three vendors differ in price, capability, and support.
procurement./practice/learnerRole: You are the Project Manager working with procurement to prepare the RFP.
procurement./practice/inputs/0: The migration has clear acceptance criteria.
procurement./practice/inputs/1: The feature backlog will be prioritized monthly.
procurement./practice/tasks/0: Divide the procurement scope based on the level of certainty.
procurement./practice/tasks/1: Propose a contract model for each portion.
procurement./practice/tasks/3: Describe how performance and changes will be managed.
procurement./practice/deliverable: A procurement strategy that includes scope packages, contract rationale, a scorecard, acceptance, and governance.
procurement./practice/guidance/0: Do not use one contract type for every portion when the risks differ.
procurement./practice/guidance/1: The weightings must reflect the business need.
procurement./practice/guidance/2: Acceptance must be measurable and linked to payment.
procurement./practice/guidance/3: The change mechanism must be sufficiently clear before the backlog changes.
procurement./practice/rubric/0/evidence: Migration and backlog use different mechanisms for clearly stated reasons.
procurement./practice/rubric/0/importance: Allocate risk to the party best able to manage it.
procurement./practice/rubric/1/evidence: Includes technical, security, delivery, support, and cost criteria.
procurement./practice/rubric/1/importance: Reduce bias toward the lowest price.
procurement./practice/rubric/2/evidence: Includes criteria, evidence, a reviewer, and a link to payment.
procurement./practice/rubric/3/evidence: Includes performance reviews, change management, and escalation.
procurement./practice/rubric/3/importance: Manage the seller throughout the engagement, not only when signing the contract.
procurement./practice/sampleAnswer/0: Use fixed price by milestone for migration; use T&M with a capacity cap and outcome reviews for the backlog.
procurement./practice/sampleAnswer/1: Score: technical 30, security 20, delivery 20, support 15, total cost 15.
procurement./practice/sampleAnswer/2: Migration payments are based on the accepted dataset and reconciliation report.
procurement./practice/sampleAnswer/3: Conduct a monthly vendor review based on SLA, defects, and forecasts; process changes through procurement authority.
procurement./practice/commonMistakes/0: Assigning price a 70% weighting that does not reflect the business need.
procurement./practice/commonMistakes/1: Failing to document data ownership and transition requirements.
procurement./practice/commonMistakes/2: Negotiating changes through chat without retaining records.
procurement./practice/reflectionQuestions/0: Which risks can the seller manage better than the buyer?
procurement./practice/reflectionQuestions/1: Which criteria protect operations after the project ends?

practice-two./opening/0: A high mock exam score does not automatically prove readiness. A learner may encounter familiar topics, make several correct guesses, or run out of time in the final block. Readiness must be assessed through trends, error types, confidence, and the ability to maintain consistent decision patterns under pressure.
practice-two./opening/1: This lesson uses a project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments as its recurring scenario. The project involves a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external vendor. The sample project is familiar enough that beginners will not get stuck on technical details, yet complex enough to demonstrate how one decision can affect multiple stakeholders. You will encounter the same group of people in later lessons; what changes is the perspective, available information, and type of decision required.
practice-two./opening/2: The final practice session is a controlled experiment: complete a mixed question set under timed conditions, record your confidence, take the planned break, and then analyze each error by cause. The goal is to determine your next learning backlog, not to impose a mandatory deadline. As you read, do not try to turn every situation into a rigid formula. Focus on the sequence of thought: understand the context, verify the information, consult the right people, assess the impact, select an action within your authority, and then update the relevant plan or documentation.

practice-two./sections/4/title: A Step-by-Step Approach for Real-World Work
practice-two./sections/4/paragraphs/0: Step 1: Prepare the timed practice session under consistent conditions so the data can be compared. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results. A step is useful only if it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
practice-two./sections/4/paragraphs/1: Step 2: Complete the questions in blocks, recording your confidence and time checkpoints. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results. A step is useful only if it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
practice-two./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Step 3: Take a short break, then review both incorrect answers and correct answers marked with low confidence. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results. A step is useful only if it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
practice-two./sections/4/paragraphs/3: Step 4: Classify the root cause and create a transfer question for each major pattern. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results. A step is useful only if it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
practice-two./sections/4/paragraphs/4: Step 5: Assess readiness using trends, timing, and remaining risk, then update the learning backlog. Clearly state the inputs, participants, and expected results. A step is useful only if it produces additional understanding, a decision, or evidence.
practice-two./sections/5/paragraphs/0: In situational questions, keywords are only signals. “Urgent” does not automatically require escalation, “the sponsor requested it” does not mean it should be implemented immediately, and “Agile” does not mean governance can be ignored. Identify the event's status, its impact, decision-making authority, and whether the question asks for the first, next, or best action.
practice-two./sections/5/paragraphs/1: If the score is high but many high-confidence answers are wrong, prioritize correcting the misconception. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value. Do not merely transfer responsibility while the Project Manager still has actions to take within their authority.
practice-two./sections/5/paragraphs/2: If you run out of time, examine the checkpoints and reading pattern before trying to increase your speed indiscriminately. Then determine whether the action produces better data, restores collaboration, or protects value. practice-two./comparison/0/useWhen: Use a scorecard and personal baseline to compare multiple sessions.
practice-two./comparison/0/projectExample: The mandatory infrastructure and integration work for the project to implement an order management platform across multiple departments, involving a business sponsor, sales and operations teams, an internal development team, and an external vendor, is baselined, while dependencies and changes are managed through the defined authority.
practice-two./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat the baseline as immutable. New information must still be analyzed and processed through change control.
practice-two./comparison/1/useWhen: Use an error backlog, small experiments, and rapid feedback.
practice-two./comparison/1/projectExample: The sales staff workflow is developed through small increments, reviewed regularly, and the backlog is adjusted using actual feedback.
practice-two./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not interpret Agile as working without structure. Objectives, roles, a Definition of Done, quality, and transparency are still required.
practice-two./comparison/2/useWhen: Keep the protocol consistent while adjusting practice topics based on error data.
practice-two./comparison/2/projectExample: Compliance milestones and data migration follow the overall plan, while the user experience is tested iteratively; the two workstreams have clear integration points.
practice-two./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not allow the two approaches to operate as separate projects that are combined only at the end. Dependencies and synchronization cadence must be managed.
